key_points: O(n + k) averagewhen input is uniformly distributed across buckets
O(n²) worst case— all elements land in one bucket → falls back to insertion sort
Best foruniformly distributed floating-point valuesin a known range
Number of buckets k is typically chosen as n (one element per bucket on average)

structural_signals.why_explanation: When input values are uniformly distributed in a known range (e.g., [0, 1)), elements spread evenly across buckets, giving O(n) average time — the ideal case for bucket sort.
The distribute-sort-merge pattern (scatter into buckets, sort each, gather results) naturally parallelizes and works well when data can be partitioned into independent ranges.

key_points: Requires sorted input(or symmetric structure like palindrome)
StartL=0, R=lastIndex, move inward based on comparison
3Sum: fix one element, two-pointer on remainder — O(n²)
Skip duplicate values to avoid repeated results

structural_signals.why_explanation: Sorted order means L+R too small → L++, too big → R--. Eliminates brute-force O(n²) pair scanning into a single O(n) pass from both ends.
Symmetric structure — compare char at L with char at R, move inward. If they always match, it's a palindrome. Natural fit for converging pointers.
Width decreases as pointers converge, so you must move the shorter wall inward — it's the only pointer that could yield a taller container. Greedy + convergent.
Fix one element, then two-pointer the sorted remainder. Sorting enables skipping duplicates and the L/R convergence reduces the inner loop from O(n) brute-force to O(n) two-pointer.
Negative numbers have large squares on the left, positives on the right. Converge from both ends, picking the larger square each step into a result array. Single pass O(n).

key_points: Slow = write position, fast = read position
Fast scans every element; slow only advances on valid writes
Preserves relative order of kept elements
Returnsslow + 1as new length

structural_signals.why_explanation: Sorted input means dupes are adjacent. Slow pointer marks the write head; fast pointer scans. When fast finds a new value, write it at slow. No extra space needed.
Slow pointer tracks where the next non-zero should go. Fast scans all elements. Non-zeros get written at slow; remaining slots are filled with zeros. In-place, order-preserving.
Need to rearrange elements by a condition (e.g., negatives before positives) without extra space. Slow marks the boundary, fast scans and swaps qualifying elements forward.
"Remove element" or "keep only matching" — fast reads every element, slow only advances when an element passes the filter. Result length = slow pointer position.
Two sorted arrays, one with trailing space. Start from the ends (largest elements) and fill backwards. Both pointers move in the same direction (right to left). O(n+m) time, O(1) extra space.
Read pointer scans runs of identical chars; write pointer writes char + count. Classic slow/fast pattern where slow = compressed write position and fast = read position.
Allow at most 2 duplicates in sorted array. Same slow/fast pointers but track count of current value.
Move even numbers to even indices, odd to odd indices. Two-pointer placement by parity.

key_points: Build first window sum over indices0..k-1
Slide:sum += arr[R] - arr[R-k]for R from k to n-1
Track max/min/count as window slides
For anagram search: compare frequency maps instead of sums

structural_signals.original_name: max sum subarray of size K
structural_signals.why_explanation: Fixed window size means each slide adds one element and removes one. Instead of recomputing the sum from scratch (O(k) per position), update incrementally in O(1).
Any property of a fixed-length substring can be maintained incrementally — add the incoming char's effect, remove the outgoing char's effect. One pass, O(n) total.
An anagram of pattern p has exactly length p. Slide a window of size |p| over s, maintaining a frequency map. When window freq matches p's freq, you found an anagram.
Take k cards from either end = total sum minus a window of size n-k. Slide the n-k window to find the minimum interior sum. Answer = total - minWindow. Fixed-size window trick.
Customers during non-grumpy minutes are guaranteed. Slide a window of size k to find where suppressing grumpiness rescues the most extra customers. Fixed window maximizes bonus.
Check if any value appears twice within a window of size k. Sliding window + set.
Fixed window of size k, check if average meets threshold. Direct sliding window sum check.

js_algorithm.name: Fixed Window
problems: Max Sum Subarray of Size K
Substring of Length K with No Repeats

key_points: ExpandR greedily,shrinkL when constraint breaks
UseHashMap<Char, Int>orSetto track window contents
Each element enters and leaves window at most once → O(n)
lastSeenmap lets L jump directly past duplicate

structural_signals.why_explanation: Window expands right greedily. When a duplicate enters, shrink left past the previous occurrence. A Set or lastSeen map tracks window contents. Each char enters/leaves once → O(n).
Expand right to satisfy the constraint (contain all target chars). Once satisfied, shrink left to minimize length. Frequency maps track "have" vs "need" counts.
Expand right freely. When distinct count exceeds K, shrink left until it drops back to K. A frequency map tracks counts; remove a key when its count hits zero.
Expand right to grow the sum. Once sum ≥ target, shrink left to find the minimum length that still satisfies. The "satisfy then minimize" pattern of variable windows.
Window tracks char frequencies. Valid if windowLen - maxFreq ≤ k (can replace the rest). Expand right always, shrink left when replacements exceed k. O(n) with frequency map.
Longest subarray with at most 2 distinct values. Variable window with a frequency map — shrink left when distinct count exceeds 2. Same as "at most K distinct" with K=2.
Shrink window when sum exceeds target. Variable-size window with sum constraint.
Longest subarray of 1s if you can flip at most k 0s. Track zero count in window.

key_points: Subarray sum = k:store prefix counts in HashMap; checkcurrentSum - k
Product except self:left-prefix product × right-suffix product
2D:inclusion-exclusion:dp[r2][c2] - dp[r1][c2] - dp[r2][c1] + dp[r1][c1]
Prefix array is sizen+1withprefix[0] = 0

structural_signals.why_explanation: Pre-build cumulative sums once O(n). Then any range [L,R] sum is prefix[R+1]-prefix[L] in O(1). Turns repeated O(n) queries into O(1) each.
If prefixSum[j] - prefixSum[i] = k, then subarray [i,j) sums to k. Store prefix sum counts in a HashMap — check if (currentSum - k) was seen before. O(n) total.
Left-prefix product gives product of all elements left of i. Right-suffix product gives all right of i. Multiply them for the answer. Avoids division, handles zeros.
Extension to 2D: build a prefix sum matrix using inclusion-exclusion. Any rectangle sum becomes O(1) with four lookups: add, subtract overlaps, add back double-subtracted corner.
Find index where left sum equals right sum. Total prefix sum lets you check leftSum == totalSum - leftSum - nums[i] at each index in O(1). Single pass after prefix build.
Replace 0→-1, then longest subarray with sum 0 = equal 0s and 1s. Use prefix sum + HashMap: if same prefix sum seen before, the subarray between is balanced.

key_points: Initialize:best = current = nums[0]
Circular variant:answer = max(standard Kadane, totalSum - minSubarray)
TrackcurrStart; updatebestStart, bestEndwhencurrent > best
If all negative: returns the least-negative element

structural_signals.why_explanation: At each position, decide: extend the current subarray or start fresh. If the running sum goes negative, it can only hurt future elements — restart. Greedy DP in O(n).
A wrapping subarray = total sum minus the minimum subarray in the middle. Run Kadane's for both max and min subarrays. Answer = max(standard, total - minSubarray).
Same Kadane's logic, but track currStart (reset when restarting) and update bestStart/bestEnd when a new best is found. Indices come for free from the extend-or-restart decision.
Track minimum price so far (buy point). At each price, profit = price - minSoFar. Same "extend or restart" idea — if price drops below minSoFar, reset the buy point.
Track both max and min running product (a negative min can flip to max). At each element: newMax = max(num, maxProd*num, minProd*num). Kadane's variant with min/max tracking.

key_points: Find first:on match, setresult=mid; R=mid-1(keep searching left)
Find last:on match, setresult=mid; L=mid+1(keep searching right)
Rotated:determine which half is sorted; narrow accordingly
Parametric:binary search on the answer — "min X s.t. predicate(X) is true"
Kotlin stdlib:arr.binarySearch(target)— returns-(insertionPoint)-1if not found

structural_signals.why_explanation: Sorted = monotonic. Compare middle element to target: if smaller, discard left half; if larger, discard right half. Halves the search space each step → O(log n).
Standard binary search stops at any match. To find the first: on match, record it but keep searching left (R=mid-1). For last: keep searching right (L=mid+1). Same O(log n).
A rotated sorted array has two sorted halves. At each step, determine which half is sorted (compare L to mid). If target falls in the sorted range, search there; otherwise search the other half.
When the answer space is monotonic ("is X feasible?" flips from false→true), binary search over the answer itself. Test the midpoint with a feasibility check. Parametric search pattern.
Not fully sorted, but locally monotonic. If mid < mid+1, a peak exists to the right; otherwise to the left. Binary search on slope direction finds any peak in O(log n).
Classic "search on answer" — binary search over eating speed k. For each k, check if Koko finishes in time (feasibility = O(n)). Monotonic: higher speed always finishes faster. O(n log max).

structural_signals.original_name: capacity to ship within D days
structural_signals.why_explanation: Binary search on capacity [max(w)..sum(w)]. For each candidate, greedily pack weights into days. Monotonic: higher capacity always needs fewer days. O(n log sum).
Find index where target would be inserted in sorted array. Classic binary search returning lo when not found.

js_algorithm.name: Binary Search
problems: Capacity to Ship Within D Days

key_points: Three pointers:low=0, mid=0, high=lastIndex
nums[mid]==0→ swap with low, advance both
nums[mid]==1→ just advance mid
nums[mid]==2→ swap with high, retreat high,don't advance mid
Single pass, in-place — no extra storage

structural_signals.why_explanation: Only 3 distinct values — don't need a full sort. Three pointers carve out regions for 0s, 1s, and 2s in a single pass. O(n) time, O(1) space — faster than O(n log n) sort.
Generalization: any time you need to split elements into exactly 3 groups (less than, equal to, greater than a pivot) in one pass. Used in quicksort optimization for duplicate-heavy data.
Elements < pivot go left, = pivot stay middle, > pivot go right. The three-pointer invariant ensures each element is examined at most once. Foundation of quicksort's partition step.
Two categories (even/odd) = simplified Dutch flag with just 2 groups. One pointer tracks the boundary, other scans — swap evens to the front. Single pass, O(1) space.
Three-way partition variant: group negatives, zeros, and positives. Same lo/mid/hi pointer technique.

key_points: Anagram:increment for s, decrement for t — all zeros = anagram
First unique:freq pass, then scan forfreq[ch] == 1
Boyer-Moore Voting:O(1) space for majority element — cancel non-matching pairs
Group anagrams:sorted chars or freq signature as map key
Kotlin:s.groupingBy { it }.eachCount()— one-liner frequency map

structural_signals.why_explanation: Anagrams have identical character frequencies. Count chars for string s (increment), then for string t (decrement). If all counts are zero, they're anagrams. O(n) time, O(1) space for fixed alphabet.
First pass: build frequency map. Second pass: scan left-to-right, return first char with count=1. Two O(n) passes. The map lets you check uniqueness in O(1) per char.
Boyer-Moore Voting: maintain a candidate and count. Same element → count++, different → count--. When count hits 0, switch candidate. The majority element survives all cancellations. O(1) space.
Count occurrences of each character/element. Foundation for anagram checks, frequency-based problems.
All anagrams share the same sorted-character key (or frequency signature). Use that as a HashMap key to bucket strings. O(n·k) where k = avg string length.
Build frequency map in O(n), then extract top K by sorting entries by count or using a min-heap of size K. The counting step is the core — heap/sort is just the extraction.

key_points: Decreasing stack:findsnext greater— pop when incoming > top
Increasing stack:findsnext smaller— pop when incoming < top
Storeindiceson stack (not values) for distance calculations
Each element enters and leaves stack at most once → amortized O(n)
Kotlin:ArrayDeque<Int>as stack —addLast()/removeLast()

structural_signals.why_explanation: For each element, you need the first larger element to its right. A decreasing stack keeps candidates. When a new element is larger than the top, it's the answer for all smaller tops — pop and record. O(n) total since each index is pushed/popped once.
"How many days until warmer?" = next greater element by index difference. Stack stores indices of unresolved days. When a warmer day arrives, pop all cooler days and compute the gap.

Water is trapped between taller bars. A decreasing stack tracks bars. When a taller bar arrives, pop shorter bars — water between the popped bar and the new taller bar fills the gap. Handles layer-by-layer.
For each bar, you need the nearest shorter bar on both sides. An increasing stack tracks bars. When a shorter bar arrives, popped bars can't extend further — compute their max rectangle. O(n) total.
"How many consecutive days was price ≤ today?" = distance to previous greater element. Decreasing stack of indices; when today's price pops smaller prices, span = today - stack.top. O(n) amortized.
Greedily remove digits that break non-decreasing order (left to right). Monotonic increasing stack: if incoming digit < top and removals left, pop the top. Ensures the smallest remaining number.
Find next smaller element for each position. Monotonic increasing stack — mirror of next greater element.

key_points: Like quicksort butonly recurse into one partition
Average O(n), worst O(n²) — use random pivot to avoid worst case
Partition places pivot at its final sorted position
If pivotIdx == k → found! If k < pivotIdx → go left, else go right
Median of Medians:guarantees O(n) worst case (rarely needed in practice)

structural_signals.why_explanation: Full sort is O(n log n) but you only need one element. Quick Select partitions like quicksort but recurses into only ONE side — the side containing k. Average O(n), only processes elements that could be the answer.
Median = element at index n/2 in sorted order. Quick Select finds this in O(n) average without sorting the entire array. Much faster than O(n log n) full sort for a single order statistic.
After Quick Select places the kth element correctly, all elements left of k are smaller (unsorted). You get the top-K partition in O(n) average — then sort just those K elements if order matters.

Maintain a min-heap of size k. Each new element: if larger than heap root, replace root and heapify. Root is always the kth largest. O(n log k) total, O(k) space. Quick Select variant for static arrays.

key_points: Key insight:values in range [1,n] → valuevbelongs at indexv-1
Swap loop:while (nums[i] != i + 1) swap(nums[i], nums[nums[i] - 1])
Skip if nums[i] == nums[target] (already placed or duplicate)
Each number moves at most once to its home → O(n) total swaps
After sorting: scan fornums[i] != i + 1to find anomalies

structural_signals.why_explanation: Array has values in range [0,n] or [1,n] — each value has a "home" index. Place each number at its home, then scan for the index where nums[i] ≠ expected. That index reveals the missing number. O(n) time, O(1) space.

Place each number at index (val-1). If nums[target] already equals the value, it's a duplicate. After one pass of cyclic placement, scan for mismatches — each mismatch is a duplicate. No extra space needed.

Ignore negatives and values > n. Place valid positives at index (val-1). After placement, the first index where nums[i] ≠ i+1 gives the first missing positive. O(n) time, O(1) space — the optimal solution.

One number duplicated, one missing (corrupt sequence). Cyclic sort places numbers at home indices. The index with a wrong number reveals both: the wrong number is the duplicate, and (index+1) is the missing one.
Place each value v at index v-1. The foundational cyclic sort operation that enables missing/duplicate detection.

key_points: Reverse words:reverse entire string, then reverse each word individually
Matrix 90° CW:transpose then reverse each row

structural_signals.why_explanation: Triple reversal trick: reverse all, reverse first k, reverse rest. Each reversal is O(n) in-place. Avoids the naive O(n·k) approach of shifting one position at a time, and uses O(1) extra space.
Reverse a subarray between two indices. Building block for rotation and word reversal algorithms.

Reverse the entire string, then reverse each word individually. The double reversal cancels out within each word but flips word order. Classic in-place string manipulation using the same reversal primitive.
Transpose (swap rows↔cols) then reverse each row = 90° clockwise. Both operations are in-place O(n²). Decomposing rotation into two simple transforms avoids complex index mapping.
Find rightmost ascending pair (i, i+1), swap nums[i] with the smallest larger element to its right, then reverse the suffix. Uses the reversal primitive for the final step. O(n) in-place.

Traverse matrix in spiral order by maintaining top/bottom/left/right boundaries. After each row/column traversal, shrink the corresponding boundary. Reversal/rotation thinking helps with direction changes.

key_points: Alwayssort by start timefirst
Insert:add before, merge overlapping, add after — O(n)
Meeting rooms:sort starts and ends separately; count overlapping starts before each end
Overlap test:current[0] <= last[1]

structural_signals.why_explanation: Sorting by start time groups overlapping intervals adjacently. Then a single greedy pass merges: if current.start ≤ last.end, they overlap — extend. Otherwise, start a new group. O(n log n) for sort.
Already sorted list: add all intervals before the new one, merge all that overlap with it, then add all after. Three phases, single pass O(n). Sorting is already done.
Sort start times and end times separately. Sweep through starts: if a meeting starts before the earliest ending, you need another room. Otherwise, reuse a freed room. Counts peak overlap.
Merge all busy intervals, then the gaps between merged intervals are the free slots. Same merge pattern, just extract the complement. Sort + merge + gap extraction.
Minimum removals to eliminate all overlaps. Sort by end time, greedily keep intervals that don't overlap with the last kept. Count of removed = total - kept. O(n log n).
Sort arrival and departure times separately. Sweep through arrivals: if an arrival comes before the earliest departure, need another platform. Same as meeting rooms — count peak concurrency.

key_points: KMP:Build LPS (failure function) in O(m), then match in O(n) — worst case O(n+m)
Rabin-Karp:Rolling hash with modular arithmetic — average O(n+m), worst O(nm)
KMP advantage:guaranteed O(n+m);Rabin-Karp advantage:simpler, multi-pattern
LPS[i]:length of longest proper prefix of pattern[0..i] that is also a suffix
Kotlin:text.indexOf(pattern)uses naive search — KMP needed for O(n+m) guarantee

structural_signals.why_explanation: Naive search is O(n·m) — backtracking on mismatch. KMP uses the failure function to skip ahead: partial matches tell you where to resume. Rabin-Karp uses rolling hash to check windows in O(1). Both achieve O(n+m).

Find ALL occurrences of a pattern in text. KMP continues scanning after each match using the LPS array. Rabin-Karp slides the hash window. Both handle overlapping matches naturally and count all instances in O(n+m).
"Does string s consist of a repeated pattern?" Build the KMP failure table. If n % (n - LPS[n-1]) == 0, the string is built from repeating the prefix of length (n - LPS[n-1]). One LPS build, O(n).

Check if B is a rotation of A by searching for B in A+A using KMP. If B appears as a substring of the doubled text, it's a rotation. Reduces rotation-check to substring search — O(n) with KMP instead of checking all n rotations naively at O(n²).

Rabin-Karp excels here: compute hashes for ALL patterns, then slide once over text checking against the hash set. Average O(n·k) for k patterns vs O(n·m·k) naive. Aho-Corasick is optimal at O(n+m) for this.

Find the longest palindromic prefix using KMP. Build LPS for (s + "#" + reversed s). The LPS value at the end tells you how much of the prefix is already a palindrome. Prepend the rest reversed. O(n).
Longest proper prefix that is also a suffix = exactly what the KMP failure function computes. LPS[n-1] gives the answer length directly. Single LPS build, O(n).

key_points: Idiomatic:groupingBy { it }.eachCount()— one-liner frequency map
Manual:freq[ch] = (freq[ch] ?: 0) + 1orfreq[ch] = freq.getOrPut(ch) { 0 } + 1
Most/least frequent:freq.maxByOrNull { it.value }
For lowercase-only:IntArray(26)indexed bych - 'a'is faster

structural_signals.why_explanation: Map stores char→count in O(n). Query any character's count in O(1). For lowercase only, IntArray(26) is faster; for Unicode or general elements, use HashMap.
Two strings are anagrams iff they have identical frequency maps. Build both maps in O(n), compare in O(k) where k = distinct chars. Or: build one map, decrement with second string, check all zeros.
Build frequency map in O(n), then sort entries by value and take top K. Or use a min-heap of size K for O(n log K). Bucket sort by frequency gives O(n) for this special case.

All anagrams share the same sorted character sequence. Use sorted string as key: getOrPut(key) { mutableListOf() }.add(word). Groups all anagrams in O(n·k log k) — or O(n·k) with frequency signature keys.

Build frequency map, then sort entries by count descending. Reconstruct string by repeating each char by its count. Bucket sort variant achieves O(n). HashMap does the counting, sort does the ordering.
Three HashSets per row/col/box track seen digits. For each cell, check if digit already in row set, col set, or box set. Box index = (r/3)*3 + c/3. O(81) = O(1) with O(1) space.

key_points: Single-pass:check complement before storing — avoids using same element twice
HashMap stores:value → index (for finding indices) or value → count (for counting pairs)
Variant:two-sum sorted array→ use two pointers O(n) + O(1) space instead

structural_signals.why_explanation: Instead of O(n²) brute force checking all pairs, store each number's index in a HashMap. For each new number, check if its complement (target - num) exists in O(1). Single pass = O(n).
Use frequency map instead of index map. For each number, add freq[target - num] to count, then increment freq[num]. Handles duplicates naturally.
Sort the array O(n log n), then for each element, use two-sum (via two pointers or HashMap) on the remainder. Skip duplicates for unique triplets. Total O(n²).
Four arrays A,B,C,D — count tuples where a+b+c+d=0. Store all a+b sums in a HashMap, then for each c+d pair check if -(c+d) exists. O(n²) time, O(n²) space. Complement lookup on pair sums.
Prefix sum + HashMap: store prefix_sum → count. At each index, check if (currentSum - k) exists in map → those are subarrays summing to k. Handles negatives unlike sliding window. O(n).

key_points: Set-based window:tracks presence — good for uniqueness constraints
Map-based window:tracks frequency — good for anagram/count constraints
Map optimization:store char → last index to jump left pointer directly
Each element enters and leaves the window at most once → amortized O(n)

structural_signals.why_explanation: A Set tracks characters in the current window. When a duplicate enters, shrink from the left until the duplicate is removed. The Set ensures O(1) membership checks. Each character enters and leaves at most once → O(n).

Two Maps: "need" stores required character counts, "window" tracks current counts. A "have" counter tracks how many characters are fully satisfied. Expand right until satisfied, then shrink left to minimize. O(n) total.
Fixed-size sliding window (size = pattern length). Compare window's frequency map to pattern's frequency map at each position. Maintain counts incrementally as window slides. O(n) total.
Map tracks char→count in window. When map.size exceeds K, shrink left, decrementing counts and removing zero-count entries. Maximizes window length with the constraint. O(n).

Fixed-size window (size = pattern length) with frequency map comparison. Slide window, maintain char counts. When window counts match pattern counts, a permutation exists. O(n) with incremental updates.
All words same length L. Slide a window of size numWords*L. Track word frequency in window map vs target map. Slide by L each step, incrementally update counts. O(n*L) total.

key_points: getOrPutis the perfect memoization primitive — returns cached or computes + stores
Data classkeys auto-generatehashCode()/equals()for multi-parameter memoization
Converts exponential recursion → O(n) time, O(n) space (one entry per unique subproblem)

structural_signals.why_explanation: Recursive problems where the same inputs are computed repeatedly (Fibonacci, grid paths, knapsack). HashMap caches results so each unique subproblem is solved only once → exponential becomes polynomial.
Any pure function (same input → same output) with costly computation benefits from caching. Store input→result in a HashMap for O(1) subsequent lookups.
DFS on graphs or trees with shared subtrees. Memoize (node, state) → result to avoid re-exploring. Use a data class key for multi-parameter state.
Classic DP: how many ways to reach step n / minimum coins for amount. Overlapping subproblems → HashMap cache turns O(2ⁿ) recursion into O(n) or O(n·k). getOrPut is the natural fit.
Can string s be segmented into dictionary words? Memoize index → boolean. At each position, try all dictionary words as prefix. Cache avoids re-checking the same suffix. O(n²·k) with memo.

key_points: LinkedHashMap approach:6-line Kotlin class — production-ready for simple cases
Manual approach:HashMap + doubly linked list with dummy head/tail sentinels
Both achieve O(1) forgetandput— the HashMap handles lookup, the linked list handles ordering
Access-order mode:third constructor paramtrueenables "move to tail on access"

structural_signals.why_explanation: When you need to cache computed results but have limited memory. LRU evicts the least recently used entry when capacity is exceeded. Both get and put are O(1), making it ideal for hot-path caching.
LRU naturally maintains a "most recently used" ordering. Perfect for "show recent files", "recent searches", or "last K accessed items". The linked list order is always up to date.
OS page replacement, CDN cache, database buffer pools — all use LRU or LRU variants. The principle: recently used data is likely to be used again soon (temporal locality).
Evict the least frequently used (ties broken by least recent). HashMap + frequency buckets (each a LinkedHashSet). More complex than LRU but better for access-skewed workloads. O(1) get/put.
Insert/delete/getMax/getMin all in O(1). HashMap for key→node lookup, doubly linked list of frequency buckets. Each bucket holds keys with that count. Similar architecture to LFU.

key_points: set.add(x)returningfalse= instant duplicate detection — the most common set idiom
toSet().sizevs original size — quick dedup check without early return
Sliding window setmaintains a fixed-size window for distance-constrained duplicates

structural_signals.why_explanation: HashSet.add() returns false if the element was already present. One pass with early return. Or one-liner: nums.toSet().size != nums.size.
Maintain a sliding window HashSet of size K. As you iterate, add the current element. If add() returns false → duplicate found within K. Remove elements that fall outside the window.
Use two sets: "seen" and "duplicates". For each element, if seen.add() returns false, add to duplicates set. O(n) time, O(n) space.
Sum of squared digits eventually loops or reaches 1. Use a HashSet to detect the cycle — if sum was seen before, it's not happy. Set membership is the cycle detection mechanism. O(1) avg per check.
Put array1 into a HashSet, then iterate array2 — if element is in the set, it's in the intersection. Remove from set to handle duplicates. O(n+m) time. Set algebra in action.

key_points: Key insight:only start counting from sequence beginnings — avoids redundant work
Each element is visited at most twice (once in scan, once in chain extension) → O(n)
Sorting would also work in O(n log n) — the HashSet approach is O(n)

structural_signals.why_explanation: Put all numbers in a HashSet. For each number, check if num-1 is NOT in the set (= start of sequence). Then extend forward: num+1, num+2, ... Each element visited at most twice → O(n).
Same start-detection trick. Count how many sequence starts exist, or track all sequence lengths. The key insight is skipping non-starts to avoid O(n²).
Put all numbers in a HashSet, then walk the expected range [lo, hi]. When a number is missing, extend the gap. Set gives O(1) membership checks for each expected value.
Follow index chains: i → nums[i] → nums[nums[i]] → ... until a cycle. Use a visited HashSet to skip already-explored indices. Each index visited at most once → O(n). Track longest cycle.

key_points: LinkedHashMap= HashMap + insertion order — iterate to find first entry with count 1
LinkedHashSetstreaming variant — maintains unique candidates in order as you scan
Both approaches: O(n) time, O(k) space where k = distinct elements

structural_signals.why_explanation: LinkedHashMap preserves insertion order. Build frequency map in one pass, then iterate entries — first with count 1 is the answer. O(n) time, O(k) space. Classic interview problem.
Every element appears twice except one. XOR approach is O(1) space, but set approach works too: add if unseen, remove if seen. The one remaining element is the single number.
Use a LinkedHashSet of candidates + a "seen" HashSet. On each element: if already seen, remove from candidates; otherwise add to both. candidates.first() is always the answer. O(1) per element.

key_points: Most fundamental LL algorithm— master this, it's a building block for many others
Alwayssavenextbefore overwritingcurr.next— losing this reference is the #1 reversal bug
Recursive version: O(n) space due to call stack — prefer iterative for production

structural_signals.why_explanation: Three-pointer technique (prev, curr, next). Save next, reverse pointer, advance. The most fundamental LL algorithm — building block for many others.
Navigate to the node before position left, then repeatedly move the next node to the front of the sublist. Dummy head handles edge case when left=1.
Find middle with fast/slow, reverse second half, compare with first half. Uses reversal as a sub-routine for O(1) space palindrome detection.
Find middle, reverse second half, then merge alternating nodes from each half. Three-step pattern: split → reverse → interleave.

key_points: TheSwiss Army knifeof linked list algorithms — used in ~40% of LL interview problems
Always use===(reference equality) when comparing nodes, not==(structural equality)
Alternative: HashSet approach is O(n) space — Floyd's is O(1) space

structural_signals.why_explanation: If there's a cycle, fast and slow must meet (gap shrinks by 1 each step). If no cycle, fast reaches null first. O(n) time, O(1) space vs O(n) space for HashSet approach.
When fast reaches the end, slow has traveled exactly half the distance. For even-length lists, check fast?.next?.next for first vs second middle.
After detecting the meeting point (Phase 1), reset one pointer to head and move both at speed 1. They meet at the cycle entry. Mathematical proof: L = kC - d.
Sum of squared digits eventually loops or reaches 1. Use fast/slow on the digit-sum sequence instead of a HashSet — O(1) space cycle detection.

key_points: Linked lists arebetter suited for merge sortthan arrays — merge step is O(1) space (just rewire pointers)
Bottom-up merge sort avoids recursion entirely — true O(1) extra space
Thedummy headpattern is essential here — avoids special-casing the first node

structural_signals.why_explanation: Dummy head + two pointers. Compare heads, attach smaller, advance that pointer. When one is exhausted, attach the remainder. O(n+m) time, O(1) space — reuses existing nodes.
Min-heap holds the head of each list. Poll smallest, attach to result, push its next into heap. O(N log k) time where N = total nodes. Or divide-and-conquer pairwise merging.
Merge sort is ideal for linked lists — find middle (fast/slow), split, recursively sort, merge. O(n log n) time, O(log n) space. No extra O(n) space needed like array merge sort.

key_points: Thedummy headhandles edge case where the head itself is removed (n = list length)
Gap between fast and slow is always n+1 — when fast=null, slow is the nodebeforethe target

structural_signals.why_explanation: Advance fast n+1 steps ahead of slow (starting from dummy). When fast reaches null, slow is just before the target. One pass, O(1) space.
Same gap technique without dummy. Advance fast by k, then move both until fast reaches null. Slow is at the kth node from the end.

key_points: When one pointer reaches null, redirect to theotherlist's head — this aligns them at the intersection
If no intersection, both become null simultaneously → returns null
Alternative: compute length difference, advance the longer list by the difference, then walk together

structural_signals.why_explanation: Virtual concatenation (A+B and B+A) aligns both pointers at the intersection. When one reaches null, redirect to the other list's head. O(n+m) time, O(1) space.
If they intersect, the two-pointer approach returns the shared node. If not, both become null simultaneously → returns null. No need to modify either list.
Alternative approach: compute length difference, advance the longer list by the difference, then walk both together until they meet. Same complexity, slightly less elegant.

key_points: Combinesfast/slow pointers(find middle) +reversal(reverse second half) +comparison
Stack approach is simpler but O(n) space — push all, then compare with a second traversal

structural_signals.why_explanation: Find middle (fast/slow), reverse second half, compare with first half. O(n) time, O(1) space. The classic three-pattern combo: fast/slow + reverse + compare.
Same middle + reverse + compare approach works whenever you need to verify the first half mirrors the second half. Stack approach is simpler but uses O(n) space.
Three-step combo: find middle (fast/slow), reverse second half, interleave both halves. O(n) time, O(1) space. The interleaving merge is the key step.
Same split + reverse + interleave pattern works whenever you need to weave elements from both ends of a list together.

key_points: Keep processing whileeither list has nodes OR carry > 0
Forward-order variant: use stacks to reverse, or reverse both lists first

structural_signals.why_explanation: Process digit by digit from least significant (head). Sum corresponding digits + carry. Dummy head builds the result list. while loop continues while either list has nodes OR carry > 0.
Use stacks to process digits from least significant, or reverse both lists first, add, then reverse the result. Stack approach avoids modifying the input.
Similar digit-by-digit processing. Multiply each digit of one number with every digit of the other, accumulating partial results with proper positional offsets and carry propagation.

key_points: Two dummy heads build two separate chains, then connect them
Must setgreater.next = null— otherwise the last node may still point to a node in the less chain, creating a cycle

structural_signals.why_explanation: Two dummy heads collect nodes into less-than and greater-than-or-equal chains. Connect them at the end. Preserves relative order within each partition. O(n) time, O(1) space.
Same two-chain pattern. Walk the list, alternately appending nodes to odd and even chains, then connect odd tail to even head. Dummy heads simplify the logic.
Extend to three dummy heads (low/mid/high) for three-way partitioning. Same connect-at-end pattern. Each node is visited exactly once.
Find length and tail, normalize k (k % length), make circular, walk to new tail at (length - k - 1), break the circle. O(n) time, O(1) space.
Same circular technique. Making the list temporarily circular simplifies finding the new head/tail without special-casing the wrap-around.
Compare current with next. If equal, skip next. If not, advance. Simple — no dummy head needed since head is always retained.
Skip ALL nodes with the duplicate value. Dummy head is needed since the head itself might be a duplicate. Track prev to reconnect after skipping.
Dummy head + three-pointer swap (prev, A, B). Rewire: prev→B→A→rest, then advance prev to A. Handles odd-length lists naturally (last node stays).
This is the k=2 special case of reverse-k-group. The simpler swap logic (3 pointer reassignments vs full reversal) makes it worth knowing separately.

key_points: Firstcheckif k nodes exist — if not, return as-is
Recursive approach is clean; iterative version avoids O(n/k) stack depth

structural_signals.why_explanation: First check if k nodes remain (return as-is if not). Reverse k nodes with the standard three-pointer technique. Recursively process the rest. O(n) time, O(n/k) stack or O(1) iterative.
Variant: reverse group 1, skip group 2, reverse group 3, etc. Same k-group reversal logic with an alternating flag to decide whether to reverse or just advance.
HashMap maps old→new nodes. Pass 1: create copies. Pass 2: wire next and random via the map. O(n) time, O(n) space. Interleaving technique achieves O(1) extra space.
Same HashMap (old→clone) pattern generalizes to any structure with arbitrary pointers. Two passes: create all nodes, then wire all edges.

key_points: One of the most important data structure combinations insystem design
HashMap gives O(1) lookup; DLL gives O(1) reordering and eviction
Sentinel head/tail eliminate all edge cases in add/remove operations

structural_signals.why_explanation: HashMap for O(1) key lookup, DLL for O(1) access-order tracking. On get: move to front. On put: add to front, evict tail if over capacity. Both operations O(1).
Extend LRU with frequency buckets. Each frequency has its own DLL. HashMap maps key → node. On access, move node to the next frequency's DLL. Evict from lowest-frequency DLL's tail.
Any scenario where you need O(1) reordering based on access/usage patterns. Browser history, recently used files, connection pools with idle timeout eviction.

key_points: Push openers, pop when closer matches top — stack empty at end = valid
Index-based variant tracks positions for length/removal calculations
HashMap of pairs makes multi-bracket matching clean and extensible

structural_signals.why_explanation: Push openers, pop when closer matches top. If mismatch or stack not empty at end, invalid. Classic stack problem.
Push indices onto stack. Use a sentinel index (-1) to calculate lengths. When matched, length = i - stack.top.
Track unmatched positions in a stack. After processing, stack size = number of removals needed.
Push opening tags, pop when closing tag matches. Same principle as bracket matching but with string tags.
Any nested open/close structure maps to the parentheses pattern. Push on open, pop on close, validate matches.

key_points: Parallel min stackmirrors the main stack — each entry stores the minimum up to that level
Alternative: store pairs(value, currentMin)in a single stack
All operations remainO(1)— the extra stack doubles space but not time

structural_signals.why_explanation: Parallel min stack tracks the minimum at each level. Push mirrors main stack with min(current, prevMin). Pop both together.
Same pattern with max instead of min. Parallel max stack tracks running maximum at each level.
Generalize to any associative operation — sum, GCD, etc. Maintain a parallel stack with the running aggregate.

key_points: Numbers push, operators pop two and push result
Order matters: popbthena, computea op b(notb op a)
Final stack contains exactly one element — the result

structural_signals.why_explanation: Numbers push, operators pop two operands and push result. No parentheses or precedence needed — order is encoded in the sequence.
Convert infix to postfix (Shunting Yard), then evaluate with a stack. Clean separation of parsing and evaluation.
Stack-based evaluation handles arbitrary expression depth naturally. Each operator consumes exactly two operands.

key_points: Each element pushed and poppedat most once=O(n)total
Decreasing stack→ next greater;increasing stack→ next smaller
Storeindices(not values) to compute distances and positions

structural_signals.why_explanation: Monotonic stack maintains sorted invariant. When an element violates the invariant, pop and assign — each element pushed and popped at most once → O(n).
Decreasing stack of indices. When a warmer day appears, pop all cooler days and compute distance.
Decreasing stack tracks previous greater element. Span = distance to that element.
Increasing stack finds left and right boundaries for each bar — the first smaller bar on each side.

key_points: Monotonic increasing stack— pop when a shorter bar appears
Sentinel 0appended at end triggers final cleanup of remaining bars
Width =i - stack.last() - 1(distance between current index and new stack top)

structural_signals.why_explanation: Monotonic increasing stack. When a shorter bar is encountered, pop taller bars and compute area. Sentinel at end forces final cleanup.
Treat each row as a histogram base. Build heights row by row, apply histogram algorithm per row. O(rows × cols).

key_points: Decreasing stack— water is trapped between the current bar and the bar below the popped top
Water per layer =width × min(left, right heights) - bottom height
Alternative: two-pointer approach usesO(1)space but stack approach generalizes to 2D

structural_signals.why_explanation: Decreasing stack. When a taller bar appears, pop and calculate water trapped between current bar and the bar below the popped one. Width × bounded height.
Two-pointer approach is more common, but stack-based thinking helps understand the bounding walls concept.

key_points: Classicmonotonic decreasing stackproblem
result[j] = i - j— distance between current warmer day and the popped cooler day
Unresolved indices remain on stack with result 0 (no warmer day exists)

structural_signals.why_explanation: Classic monotonic decreasing stack. Push indices of unresolved days. When a warmer day appears, pop and compute distance.
Generalization: any "how far until condition X" maps to a monotonic stack with distance calculation.

key_points: Split by"/", push directory names,".."pops,"."and""skip
Result ="/" + stack.joinToString("/")
Edge case:".."at root does nothing (stack is already empty)

structural_signals.why_explanation: Split by "/", push directory names, ".." pops (go up), "." and "" are no-ops. Join stack with "/" for canonical path.
Stack naturally models directory hierarchy — push to descend, pop to ascend.

key_points: '['pushes current state (count + string built so far),']'pops and repeats
Two stacks:counts(repetition factors) andstrings(partial results)
Handles arbitrary nesting via stack depth — inner patterns resolve first

structural_signals.why_explanation: Two stacks (counts + strings) handle nesting. '[' pushes current state, ']' pops and repeats. Natural recursive structure flattened to iterative.
Each '[' starts a new scope. Stack depth = nesting level. Inner patterns resolve first (LIFO matches nesting).

key_points: Sign tracking:'('pushes result + sign,')'pops and applies
Calculator II:*and/process immediately;+and-defer to final sum
Combine both for a full calculator with parentheses and precedence

structural_signals.why_explanation: '(' pushes current result + sign onto stack, ')' pops and combines. Parentheses create scopes that the stack manages naturally.
* and / process immediately (pop, compute, push). + and - defer by pushing. Final sum of stack gives result. Higher precedence = immediate processing.

key_points: Collision only whenstack top > 0andcurrent < 0(moving toward each other)
Compare absolute values: larger survives, equal both explode
Non-colliding asteroids (same direction, or left-then-right) just push onto stack

structural_signals.why_explanation: Stack simulates collisions: positive = right, negative = left. Collision only when top is positive and current is negative. Compare absolute values to determine survivor.
Any problem where elements moving in opposite directions cancel each other. Stack tracks surviving elements.

key_points: Monotonic increasing stack— greedily remove digits greater than the next one
If removals remain after scanning, remove from the end (largest digits in increasing sequence)
Trim leading zeros— essential post-processing step

structural_signals.why_explanation: Monotonic increasing stack. Greedily remove digits that are greater than the next digit — this produces the smallest possible number.
Generalization: build the lexicographically smallest/largest sequence by greedily maintaining monotonic order with a removal budget.

key_points: Recursion = implicit stack— any recursive DFS can be made iterative with an explicit stack
For graphs: push neighbors inreversedorder so left/first neighbor is processed first
Inorder tree traversal: go left as far as possible, then process node, then go right

structural_signals.why_explanation: Explicit stack replaces the call stack. Avoids StackOverflowError on deep graphs/trees. Same time complexity as recursive DFS.
JVM default stack depth ~5000-10000 frames. For trees with depth > 5000, iterative DFS is required.

structural_signals.original_name: dfs on deep chain (avoid stack overflow)
structural_signals.why_explanation: Heap memory is much larger than stack memory. Explicit stack uses heap, supporting millions of elements.

js_algorithm.name: DFS with Stack
problems: DFS on Deep Chain (Avoid Stack Overflow)

key_points: Monotonic decreasing stack— pop all elements ≤ current price
Span =current index - previous greater element's index
Online variant stores (price, span) pairs — no need for index tracking

structural_signals.why_explanation: Monotonic decreasing stack. Span = number of consecutive days with price ≤ today's price. Pop all smaller/equal, span = distance to remaining top.
Same as stock span — the popped elements represent consecutive days that don't exceed the current price.
The element remaining on top of the stack after popping is the previous greater element. Span = distance to it.

key_points: THE canonical queue algorithm— BFS is to queues what DFS is to stacks
Explores all nodes at distancedbefore distanced+1
Always mark visited when ENQUEUING, not when processing — prevents duplicate enqueues

structural_signals.why_explanation: BFS explores by distance. All neighbors at distance d are visited before d+1, so the first time you reach a node is the shortest path.
Start BFS from each unvisited land cell to flood-fill the entire island. Each BFS = one connected component.
Each BFS level = one move. First arrival at target = fewest moves. Works for grid puzzles, state-space search.
BFS radiates outward uniformly. The first target found is guaranteed to be the closest one.
BFS visits every node exactly once. Use a HashMap to map original→clone, wiring neighbors as you go.
BFS with 2-coloring. Alternate colors at each level. If a neighbor has the same color → not bipartite.

key_points: queue.sizesnapshot is the critical technique— captures how many nodes belong to the current level
Process exactlylevelSizenodes before moving on — children enqueued during this loop belong to thenextlevel

structural_signals.why_explanation: Process all nodes at depth d before depth d+1 using queue.size snapshot per level.
Same as level-order but reverse every other level. Collect level, conditionally reverse.
The last (or first) node processed in each level is the rightmost (or leftmost) visible node.
BFS finds the first leaf node — that's the shallowest leaf = minimum depth. Faster than DFS which must explore all paths.
Process each level, compute sum/count. Level snapshot ensures clean separation.
Track max while processing each level. One pass per level, no sorting needed.

key_points: Enqueue ALL sources initially— equivalent to adding a virtual super-source connected to all start points
BFS radiates from all sourcesin parallel— each layer = one time step
Same complexity as single-source BFS:O(V+E)

structural_signals.why_explanation: All rotten oranges start spreading simultaneously. Enqueue all rotten cells, BFS radiates in parallel — each level = 1 minute.
Enqueue all gates, BFS fills each empty room with distance to nearest gate. Multi-source = all gates radiate simultaneously.
Virtual super-source connected to all start points with 0-weight edges. One BFS covers all.
Enqueue all 0-cells, BFS outward. Each 1-cell gets filled with its distance to the nearest 0 on first visit.
Reverse multi-source BFS from each ocean border inward. Cells reachable from both oceans are the answer.

key_points: Model asimplicit graph— words differing by 1 letter are connected by an edge
BFS finds theshortest transformation sequence
Bidirectional BFS reduces search space fromO(bd)toO(bd/2)

structural_signals.why_explanation: Implicit graph: words are nodes, edges connect words differing by 1 letter. BFS finds shortest transformation.
State = lock combination (4-digit string). Each turn = 1 edge. BFS from "0000" to target avoiding deadends.
Same as word ladder but with gene strings (ACGT). BFS through valid mutations in the gene bank.
State = board configuration. Each move = swap blank with neighbor. BFS through state space for shortest solution.

key_points: Deque storesindicesin decreasing value order — not values directly
Remove expiredfrom front (index outside window),remove smallerfrom back (maintain monotonicity)
Front always holds thecurrent window maximum

structural_signals.why_explanation: Monotonic decreasing deque: front = window max. Remove expired from front, remove smaller from back. O(n) total.
Same pattern but monotonic increasing deque. Front = window min.

structural_signals.original_name: max in all subarrays of size k
structural_signals.why_explanation: Identical to sliding window maximum. Output result when window reaches size k.
Monotonic deque on prefix sums. Remove from front when valid, from back when not useful. Handles negative numbers.
DP + monotonic deque: dp[i] = nums[i] + max(dp[j]) for j in window. Deque tracks max dp value in sliding window.

js_algorithm.name: Sliding Window Max
problems: Max in All Subarrays of Size K

key_points: Makepush O(n)by rotating elements — ensures front of queue = top of stack
Single-queue variant rotatessize - 1elements after each push

structural_signals.why_explanation: Classic data structure design question. Tests understanding of LIFO vs FIFO inversion. Push-heavy or pop-heavy variants.

key_points: Each element moved at mosttwice(push stack → pop stack) — amortizedO(1)per operation
Lazy transfer:only pour push stack into pop stack when pop stack is empty

structural_signals.why_explanation: Classic design question. Tests amortized analysis. Two-stack lazy transfer achieves O(1) amortized per operation.

key_points: Queue maintains awindow of k values— evicts oldest when full
Running sumavoids recomputation — add new, subtract evicted =O(1)per call

structural_signals.why_explanation: Queue holds the last k values. On each new value: enqueue, dequeue oldest if over k. Running sum gives O(1) average.
Queue of timestamps. Dequeue expired hits (older than 5 minutes). Queue size = hit count in window.
Queue of request timestamps. If queue size ≥ limit within time window → reject. Dequeue expired entries.
Queue stores call timestamps. On each call, dequeue entries older than 3000ms. Queue size = count.

key_points: 0-weightedges →addFirst(front),1-weightedges →addLast(back)
O(V+E)vs Dijkstra'sO((V+E) log V)— no heap overhead needed

structural_signals.why_explanation: Grid cells have direction arrows. Following costs 0, changing costs 1. 0/1 weights → 01-BFS instead of Dijkstra.
When movement costs are 0 (same terrain) or 1 (obstacle bypass), 01-BFS is O(V+E) vs Dijkstra's O((V+E) log V).
Deque replaces priority queue: 0-weight → front (same distance), 1-weight → back (one more). Linear time.

key_points: Greedily schedule most frequent task— max-heap ensures optimal ordering
Cooldown queue holds tasks waiting to become available — dequeued when their time arrives
Max-heap + cooldown queue = greedy scheduling with constraints

structural_signals.why_explanation: Greedy: always run the most frequent available task. Max-heap picks it, cooldown queue holds it until ready. Minimizes idle slots.
Same pattern: pick highest priority ready task, enforce minimum gap between same-type tasks.
Place most frequent char, wait k positions before reusing. Max-heap + cooldown queue enforces the gap.

key_points: Min-heap of size k— when heap exceeds k, poll smallest; what remains are the top k
Bucket sort alternative isO(n)— use when k is large relative to n

structural_signals.why_explanation: Count frequencies, then min-heap of size k. Poll smallest when over k. What remains = top k. O(n log k).
Same pattern but with custom comparator for alphabetical tie-breaking.
Count frequencies, max-heap by frequency, build result by polling. O(n log k) where k = distinct chars.
Max-heap of size k by distance. Poll farthest when over k. Remaining k points are closest. O(n log k).

key_points: Min-heap of size kfor kth largest — root = answer after all elements processed
For merge-k: heap holdsheads of each list, poll smallest and push its next node

structural_signals.why_explanation: Min-heap of size k. After all elements: root = kth largest. O(n log k) — better than full sort when k ≪ n.
Same min-heap of size k, maintained as elements arrive. Root always = current kth largest. O(log k) per add.
Min-heap of k list heads. Poll smallest, push its successor. Always merging the global minimum. O(N log k).
Min-heap with (value, row, col). Poll k times, each time pushing the next element from same row. O(k log k).
Min-heap of (a[i]+b[j], i, j). Poll and expand neighbors. Lazily explores pairs in sorted order.

key_points: maxHeapholds smaller half,minHeapholds larger half
Invariant:sizes differ by at most 1 — rebalance after each insertion
Median = top of larger heap (odd) or average of both tops (even)

structural_signals.why_explanation: Two heaps split data into halves. maxHeap top = largest of small half, minHeap top = smallest of large half. Median from tops.
Same two-heap approach but with lazy deletion. Track elements to remove, clean up when they reach heap top.
Any problem where you need O(1) access to the "middle" of a sorted stream. Percentile tracking, balanced partitions.

key_points: Min-heap PQalways processes the closest unvisited node — greedy and optimal
Skip already-visited nodes—if (d > dist[u]) continuehandles stale entries
DoesNOTwork with negative weights — use Bellman-Ford for negative edges

structural_signals.why_explanation: Min-heap PQ processes closest unvisited node. With non-negative weights, settled distance is final. O((V+E) log V).
Dijkstra from source, find max of all shortest distances. If any node unreachable → return -1.
Modified Dijkstra/Bellman-Ford with stop limit. Track (cost, node, stops) in priority queue.
Edge weight = abs height difference. Dijkstra finds path minimizing the maximum single-step effort.
Grid cells have elevation. Min-heap BFS: always expand the cell with lowest elevation. First to reach (n-1,n-1) = answer.

key_points: Greedily place most frequent char— max-heap ensures optimal selection
Holdprevious char asideuntil next iteration — prevents adjacent duplicates
Impossible ifmaxFreq > (n+1)/2

structural_signals.why_explanation: No two adjacent chars the same. Max-heap greedily picks most frequent available char. Hold previous aside to prevent repeats.
Same pattern: rearrange array so no two adjacent elements are equal. Max-heap by frequency, alternate placement.
Any problem requiring "spread out" the most frequent element. If maxFreq > (n+1)/2 → impossible.

key_points: maxDepth is simple recursion: max(left, right) + 1
minDepth must handle nodes with only one child
BFS finds min depth faster — stops at first leaf

structural_signals.why_explanation: Classic bottom-up: max(left, right) + 1. Base case null → 0. Every tree height question uses this.
Shortest root-to-leaf path. Must handle single-child nodes (not leaves). BFS is more efficient — stops at first leaf.
Height at each node feeds into |left - right| ≤ 1 check. Combines depth with validation.
Depth calculation is the foundation. Level = depth + 1. Count by tracking depth during traversal.

key_points: At each node, diameter through it = height(left) + height(right)
May not pass through root — track global max

structural_signals.why_explanation: Longest path between any two nodes. At each node: height(left) + height(right). Track global max — path may not go through root.
Same as diameter. Bottom-up height calculation with global max tracking.
Diameter = farthest pair. One DFS computes all heights and tracks max left+right sum.

key_points: Balanced check returns -1 to short-circuit — avoids O(n²)
Symmetric compares left.left↔right.right and left.right↔right.left

structural_signals.why_explanation: Return height or -1 (unbalanced). One DFS, O(n). Avoids the naive O(n²) of computing height separately at each node.
Compare left subtree with mirror of right. Recursive: left.left↔right.right AND left.right↔right.left.
BFS level-order: once a non-full node is seen, all subsequent nodes must be leaves.

key_points: Swap left↔right recursively at every node
Iterative version uses queue — same O(n) time

structural_signals.why_explanation: Swap left↔right at every node, recurse. The canonical "simple tree recursion" interview question.
Identical to invert. Create mirror image for comparison or display purposes.
Two trees are flip-equivalent if one can become the other by swapping children at some nodes. Recursive check with both swap/no-swap options.

key_points: Path Sum III uses prefix sum + HashMap (like subarray sum)
Backtrack by removing last element after exploring children

structural_signals.why_explanation: Pass remaining target down. At leaf: check if remaining == 0. Classic top-down parameter passing.
Same as path sum but collect ALL paths. Backtrack: add node to path, recurse, remove after.
Prefix sum + HashMap on tree paths. If currentSum - target exists in map → valid subpath. Backtrack map entry after subtree.
Related pattern: at each node compute best path through it (left + right + node). Report one branch up to parent.
Each root-to-leaf path forms a number. Pass accumulated value × 10 + node.val down. Sum at leaves.

key_points: Binary tree LCA is O(n) — must check both subtrees
BST LCA is O(h) — follow the split point where p and q diverge

structural_signals.why_explanation: If p in left subtree and q in right → current node is LCA. If both same side → LCA is deeper. O(n) for binary tree.
BST ordering: if both < node go left, both > go right, else split point = LCA. O(h) — much faster than general LCA.
Find LCA, then dist(p,q) = dist(root,p) + dist(root,q) - 2×dist(root,LCA). LCA is the key step.
Like intersection of two linked lists: walk both pointers up, they meet at LCA. O(h) time, O(1) space.

key_points: Preorder with null markers (#) — BFS variant also works
Deserialize consumes tokens from queue in same order

structural_signals.why_explanation: Convert tree ↔ string for storage, transmission, or comparison. Preorder + null markers preserves structure completely.
Serialize N-ary to string, deserialize as binary (left-child right-sibling). Same serialize/deserialize pattern.
Serialize both trees, compare strings. Useful for subtree matching — is serialized(t) a substring of serialized(s)?

key_points: Preorder's first element = root, find root in inorder to split left/right
HashMap for O(1) index lookup in inorder array

structural_signals.why_explanation: Preorder[0] = root. Find root in inorder → everything left = left subtree, right = right subtree. Recurse. O(n) with HashMap.
Postorder[last] = root. Same split logic but process right subtree first (postorder visits right before root).
Only unique for full binary trees. Preorder[1] = left root, find it in postorder to determine left subtree size.

key_points: BFS with level size snapshot — take last node per level for right side view
DFS alternative: visit right subtree first, take first node at each new depth

structural_signals.why_explanation: BFS: last node in each level is the rightmost visible. Or DFS right-first: first node at each new depth is the answer.
BFS level-by-level, reverse every other level. Queue.size snapshot separates levels cleanly.
Standard level-order then reverse the result list. Or collect levels and return reversed.
BFS level snapshot: track max per level. Single pass, no sorting needed.

key_points: Find rightmost node of left subtree, connect to right subtree
Move left subtree to right, nullify left — O(1) space

structural_signals.why_explanation: Convert tree to right-pointer-only chain in preorder. Morris-style: find rightmost of left subtree, link to right subtree. O(1) space.
Inorder traversal linking prev↔current. Similar flatten but with bidirectional links.
Flatten first, then iterate right pointers. Trades tree structure for simple iteration.

key_points: Inorder traversal stops at kth element — no need to visit all nodes
BST iterator uses controlled inorder with stack — O(h) space, O(1) amortized next()

structural_signals.why_explanation: Inorder traversal of BST = sorted order. Stop at kth element. O(h + k) time.
Controlled inorder using a stack: pushAllLeft, then pop+process+pushAllLeft(right). O(h) space, O(1) amortized next().
Reverse inorder (right→root→left). Stop at kth element. Same technique, opposite direction.
Binary search down the tree tracking closest so far. O(h) — no need for full traversal.

key_points: Max path sum: node can use both branches locally but only contributes one to parent
Ignore negative gains — maxOf(0, gain) to prune bad paths

structural_signals.why_explanation: At each node: pathSum = val + leftGain + rightGain (both branches). Report upward = val + max(left, right) (one branch). Track global max.
Pass maxSoFar top-down. Node is "good" if its value ≥ maxSoFar on the path from root.
Similar to diameter: at each node compute matching-value path through it. Track global max. Bottom-up return.
DP on tree: each node returns (rob, skip). Rob = val + skip(left) + skip(right). Skip = max of children's rob/skip.

key_points: Same tree is the base comparison for subtree check
Subtree checks isSameTree at every node — O(m*n) worst case
Merge sums overlapping node values

structural_signals.why_explanation: Recursively compare: both null → true. One null → false. Values equal AND both subtrees same → true. O(min(n,m)).
At every node of s, check if subtree rooted there equals t (using isSameTree). O(m×n) brute force, O(m+n) with serialization.
Overlapping nodes: sum values. Non-overlapping: take whichever exists. Natural recursive structure.
Collect leaf sequences via DFS on both trees, compare the sequences. Uses same isSameTree building block.

key_points: Vertical order uses column tracking with BFS + TreeMap
Sorted array → BST picks middle as root recursively

structural_signals.why_explanation: Assign column to each node (root=0, left=col-1, right=col+1). BFS + TreeMap groups by column. Sort by row within column.
Middle element = root ensures balance. Recurse on left half and right half. O(n), produces minimum-height BST.
Same middle-as-root idea but use slow/fast pointers to find middle. Or inorder simulation with advancing pointer.
Left boundary (top-down) + leaves (DFS) + right boundary (bottom-up). Combines vertical/level thinking.

key_points: O(L) per operation where L = word length
Each root-to-node path = a prefix
Array children (26 slots) — fast but memory-heavy; HashMap children — flexible character set

structural_signals.why_explanation: startsWith(prefix) in O(L). Then DFS from prefix node to enumerate all completions. Foundation of search bars.
Build trie from word list, DFS the board following trie edges. Prune branches not in trie — dramatically faster than checking each word.
Insert all strings, walk trie until a node has >1 child or is end-of-word. Path so far = longest common prefix.
O(L) exact lookup + prefix search. Can also find words within edit distance by exploring adjacent trie branches.
Insert all suffixes of string into trie. Number of trie nodes = number of distinct substrings.
Binary trie (bit-by-bit). For each number, greedily take opposite bit at each level to maximize XOR.

key_points: Array of 4n size — build O(n), query O(log n), update O(log n)
Lazy propagation for efficient range updates

structural_signals.why_explanation: Query any range [l,r] in O(log n). Supports sum, min, max, GCD — any associative operation. Build in O(n).
Lazy propagation: defer updates to children until needed. Both range update and range query in O(log n).
Merge-sort tree variant or segment tree with sorted lists at nodes. Count elements in [lo, hi] within index range [l, r].
Segment tree on timeline: update ranges (add/remove intervals), query point for active count.

key_points: Simpler than segment tree (~15 lines), O(n) space
Uses lowest set bit trick:i & (-i)
Only supports sum — cannot do min/max like segment tree

structural_signals.why_explanation: O(log n) update + O(log n) prefix sum query. Simpler and faster constant than segment tree for sum-only problems.
Process elements right-to-left (or by value), BIT tracks how many smaller elements seen so far. O(n log n).
rangeSum(l, r) = prefixSum(r) - prefixSum(l-1). Point update O(log n). Much simpler than segment tree.
Process right-to-left, BIT indexed by value. Query prefix sum up to current value = count of smaller elements to the right.
BFS naturally finds shortest paths because it explores layer by layer. Each level = one more edge.
Queue processes one complete layer before the next. Perfect for tree level-order, minimum moves.
BFS finds the closest target first. Stop as soon as target is dequeued.
Start from multiple sources simultaneously. Classic: rotten oranges, 01-matrix.
Visit all reachable nodes in breadth-first order. Foundation for shortest path and level-order processing.
Back edges in DFS tree indicate cycles. Use three-color for directed graphs.
DFS reverse postorder gives topological ordering of a DAG.
Each DFS from an unvisited vertex discovers one component.
DFS explores full paths. Good for "can we reach X from Y?"
DFS naturally explores, undoes, and tries alternatives.
Visit all reachable nodes in depth-first order. Foundation for cycle detection, topological sort, and connected components.
Each BFS/DFS from an unvisited vertex = one component. O(V+E).
Use Union-Find when edges arrive one at a time. O(E × α(V)) ≈ O(E).
Adding an edge between nodes in the same component creates a cycle. Union-Find detects this in near O(1) per edge.
DFS parent check or Union-Find. Visited non-parent neighbor = cycle.
Three-color DFS (back edge to GRAY node) or Kahn's topological sort (processed < n means cycle).
Course schedule, build order, prerequisites. Kahn's also detects cycles.
Modules must be compiled after their dependencies. Topological sort gives valid order.
Greedy with min-heap. Process each vertex once. Standard for weighted graphs.
Find max of all shortest distances = time for signal to reach all nodes.
Track predecessor array during Dijkstra to reconstruct the actual shortest path, not just the distance.
Dijkstra fails with negatives. Bellman-Ford relaxes all edges V-1 times.
If V-th iteration still relaxes, a negative cycle exists.
Modified Bellman-Ford: relax only k+1 times, using previous iteration's distances.
Sort edges by weight, greedily add if no cycle (Union-Find). Best for sparse graphs.
Grow MST from a starting node using a priority queue. Best for dense graphs.
Each DFS/BFS from unvisited '1' discovers one island. Mark visited to avoid re-counting.
Same DFS, but return the count of cells visited. Track maximum.

key_points: dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2] — the fundamental recurrence
Generalize to k steps with inner loop over step sizes
O(1) space with rolling variables

structural_signals.why_explanation: dp[n] = dp[n-1] + dp[n-2]. The canonical DP example. Memo turns O(2^n) → O(n). Rolling vars → O(1) space.
Identical to fibonacci: ways to reach step n = ways to reach n-1 + ways to reach n-2. dp[i] = dp[i-1] + dp[i-2].
dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]). Same recurrence but with a cost layer on top.
Generalize: dp[i] = sum of previous k values. Inner loop over step sizes.

key_points: dp[i] = max(skip=dp[i-1], rob=dp[i-2]+nums[i])
Circular version splits into two linear problems: exclude first or last

structural_signals.why_explanation: Can't pick adjacent elements. dp[i] = max(skip, take). Classic "include/exclude with adjacency constraint."
First and last are adjacent. Solve twice: exclude first OR exclude last. Max of both.
DP on tree: return (rob, skip) pair per node. Rob = val + skip(children). Skip = max(rob, skip) per child.
Reduce to house robber: count[v] × v = "value at house v". Can't pick adjacent values → same recurrence.
Same take/skip pattern with constraint. dp tracks best ending with include vs exclude.

key_points: Min coins: dp[a] = min(dp[a - coin] + 1)
Count ways: coins outer loop → combinations; amounts outer → permutations

structural_signals.why_explanation: dp[amount] = min over coins of dp[amount - coin] + 1. Unbounded knapsack variant — each coin reusable.
Coins outer, amounts inner → combinations (order doesn't matter). Amounts outer, coins inner → permutations.
Count ordered combinations summing to target. Amounts outer loop — treats [1,2] and [2,1] as different.
dp[day] = min cost to cover travel through day. Try 1-day, 7-day, 30-day passes at each travel day.

key_points: O(n²) checks all j < i where nums[j] < nums[i]
O(n log n) maintains tails array with binary search replacement

structural_signals.why_explanation: dp[i] = LIS ending at i. O(n²) DP or O(n log n) with patience sorting (tails + binary search).
Track both length and count at each position. When dp[j]+1 == dp[i], add count[j] to count[i].
Sort by second element, then LIS on first elements. Or greedy like activity selection.
Sort by width ascending (height descending for ties), then LIS on heights. 2D LIS reduction.
Same as LIS but use ≤ instead of <. Binary search uses upper_bound instead of lower_bound.

key_points: dp[i] = max(nums[i], dp[i-1]+nums[i]) — extend or restart
Product variant needs min tracking because negative × negative = positive

structural_signals.why_explanation: Kadane's: dp[i] = max(nums[i], dp[i-1]+nums[i]). Extend current subarray or start fresh. O(n), O(1) space.
Track both max AND min at each position. Negative × negative = positive, so min can become max.
Answer = max(normal Kadane, totalSum - minSubarray). Circular wrap = total minus the minimum contiguous segment.
Kadane left-to-right + Kadane right-to-left. Best split = max(leftMax[i] + rightMax[i+1]).

key_points: Decode ways is similar to climbing stairs with constraints on valid codes
Word break checks all prefixes in dictionary at each position

structural_signals.why_explanation: Like climbing stairs but only valid 1-2 digit codes (1-26). dp[i] += dp[i-1] if single valid, += dp[i-2] if double valid.
dp[i] = can s[0..i-1] be segmented? Check all j: dp[j] && s[j..i] in dict. O(n² × L).
Same DP + backtracking to collect all valid segmentations. Memoize to avoid re-exploring.
Partition string into 4 valid octets (0-255). DP/backtracking with validity constraints at each split.

key_points: Greedy tracks farthest reachable index
Min jumps uses BFS levels — increment jumps when current level ends
DP approach is O(n²) — greedy is optimal here

structural_signals.why_explanation: Greedy: track farthest reachable. If current index > farthest → stuck. O(n), O(1).
BFS-level greedy: each "level" = one jump. Track currentEnd and farthest. Increment jumps when i == currentEnd.
DP with HashSet per stone: track which jump sizes can reach it. For each stone, try k-1, k, k+1.

key_points: dp[n] = min(dp[n - k²] + 1) for all valid k
Same structure as coin change where coins are perfect squares

structural_signals.why_explanation: dp[n] = min(dp[n-k²]+1). Identical to coin change where "coins" are 1,4,9,16,... O(n√n).
DP generating sequence: track pointers for ×2, ×3, ×5. Next ugly = min of three candidates.
dp[n] = max(j × (n-j), j × dp[n-j]) for all j. Break n into parts that maximize product.

key_points: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1] for path counting
Obstacles set dp to 0
Optimize to single row since each cell depends only on current and previous row

structural_signals.why_explanation: Count paths from top-left to bottom-right (right/down only). dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]. O(m×n), optimizable to O(n).
Same recurrence but dp[i][j] = 0 if grid[i][j] is obstacle.
dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Same grid, minimize instead of count.
Bottom-up: dp[i][j] += min(dp[i+1][j], dp[i+1][j+1]). Triangular grid DP.
dp[i][j] = min(left, top, diagonal) + 1 if cell is '1'. Track largest square of all 1s.

key_points: Match → dp[i-1][j-1]+1, mismatch → max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1])
Reconstruct by backtracking through the table

structural_signals.why_explanation: Two strings, find longest shared subsequence. Match → diagonal+1, mismatch → max(skip from either). O(m×n).
Length = m + n - LCS. Reconstruct by interleaving both strings, sharing LCS characters.
= n - LPS(s). LPS = LCS(s, reverse(s)). Insert the non-palindromic chars.
LCS of two file versions = unchanged lines. Differences = lines not in LCS.

key_points: Three operations: insert, delete, replace
Match → dp[i-1][j-1], else → 1 + min(delete, insert, replace)

structural_signals.why_explanation: Min insert/delete/replace to transform s1→s2. dp[i][j] = cost for s1[0..i-1]↔s2[0..j-1]. O(m×n).
Simplified: are two strings exactly one edit apart? Compare lengths, scan for the single difference.
Same DP structure but cost = ASCII values of deleted chars instead of count.
Count ways s contains t as subsequence. Match → dp[i-1][j-1]+dp[i-1][j], skip → dp[i-1][j].
Rank dictionary words by edit distance to misspelled word. Edit distance is the core metric.

key_points: RIGHT-TO-LEFT for 0/1 (each item once) — this is the #1 DP pitfall
LEFT-TO-RIGHT for unbounded (item reuse)

structural_signals.why_explanation: Each item used at most once. dp[w] iterated RIGHT-TO-LEFT so each item isn't reused within the same pass.
Items reusable. dp[w] iterated LEFT-TO-RIGHT — allows same item to contribute multiple times.
Assign + or - to each number. Reduces to 0/1 knapsack: find subsets summing to (target+total)/2.
Partition stones into two groups minimizing |sum1-sum2|. = 0/1 knapsack with target total/2.
2D knapsack: capacity is (m zeros, n ones). Each string costs (zeros, ones). Max strings fitting.

key_points: Subsequence: LCS(s, reverse(s)) or interval DP
Substring: expand-around-center for O(1) space

structural_signals.why_explanation: LPS(s) = LCS(s, reverse(s)). Or interval DP: dp[i][j] = LPS of s[i..j]. If s[i]==s[j] → dp[i+1][j-1]+2.
DP: dp[i][j] = true if s[i..j] is palindrome. Or expand-around-center: O(n²) time, O(1) space.
dp[i] = min cuts for s[0..i]. Precompute isPalin[i][j], then dp[i] = min(dp[j-1]+1) for palindromic s[j..i].
Expand from each center (odd and even length). Count all expansions that remain palindromic.

key_points: * in regex matches zero or more of preceding element
* in wildcard matches any sequence of characters
Both need careful base cases for empty patterns

structural_signals.why_explanation: '.' = any char, '*' = zero+ of preceding. dp[i][j] = does s[0..i-1] match p[0..j-1]? Handle '*' as zero or more.
'?' = any single char, '*' = any sequence. dp[i][j]: '*' → dp[i-1][j] || dp[i][j-1].
Can s3 be formed by interleaving s1 and s2? dp[i][j] = s3[i+j-1] matches s1[i-1] or s2[j-1] from valid state.

key_points: Boolean knapsack — right-to-left for 0/1 constraint
Partition = subset sum of total/2
Target sum reduces to subset sum via algebra

structural_signals.why_explanation: Can a subset sum to target? dp[j] = dp[j] || dp[j-num]. Boolean 0/1 knapsack. O(n × target).
Can array be split into two equal-sum halves? Equivalent to subset sum with target = total/2.
Assign +/- to each number to reach target. P-N=target, P+N=total → P=(target+total)/2. Count subsets summing to P.
Bitmask DP or backtracking. Track which elements used (mask) and current bucket sum modulo target.

key_points: dp[i][j] = best answer for range [i..j]
Try all split points k between i and j
Fill by increasing interval length

structural_signals.why_explanation: dp[i][j] = min cost to multiply matrices i..j. Try all split points k. O(n³).
Think about which balloon to burst LAST in range [i,j]. dp[i][j] = max coins from range. O(n³).
Merge k consecutive piles. dp[i][j] = min cost. Only mergeable when (j-i) % (k-1) == 0.
dp[i][j] = min cuts for s[i..j]. Interval DP with palindrome precomputation.
Given search frequencies, minimize expected search cost. Try each key as root in range [i..j]. Classic interval DP.

key_points: Define states (hold, sold, rest) and transitions between them
Write transition equations — each state depends on previous states
Single transaction is simply tracking min price seen so far

structural_signals.why_explanation: Track min price so far. Profit = price - minSoFar. O(n), O(1). Not really DP — just greedy scan.
Capture every upward move. Greedy: profit += max(0, price[i] - price[i-1]).

structural_signals.original_name: best time: at most k transactions
structural_signals.why_explanation: dp[t][i] = max profit using t transactions by day i. Track maxDiff for O(k×n) optimization.
State machine: hold/sold/rest. hold[i] = max(hold[i-1], rest[i-1]-price). sold[i] = hold[i-1]+price. rest[i] = max(rest[i-1], sold[i-1]).
Two states: cash and hold. cash = max(cash, hold+price-fee). hold = max(hold, cash-price).

js_algorithm.name: State Machine DP \u2014 Stocks
problems: Best Time: At Most K Transactions

key_points: Mask represents which elements are visited/used as a subset
dp[mask][i] = best result ending at i with mask visited
Only works for small n (≤ 20) due to exponential states

structural_signals.why_explanation: dp[mask][i] = min cost visiting cities in mask, ending at i. O(2^n × n²). Only feasible for n ≤ 20.
